medications.indication_rationale: First-line topical antifungal reduces Malassezia load, the driver of the inflammatory flare; apply to affected facial areas.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to affected facial areas twice daily until clear (as stated in the source note)

medications.indication_rationale: A short course of a MILD topical corticosteroid settles the acute inflammation and burning; face-appropriate low potency limits atrophy risk.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply sparingly once daily to the face for a short course of about 5 days (as stated in the source note)
medications.deprescribing_note: Stop after the short flare course; do not continue mild steroid open-endedly on the face — prolonged facial use risks atrophy, telangiectasia and perioral dermatitis.

medications.indication_rationale: Treats the scalp seborrhoea/dandruff component; leave on before rinsing to allow contact time.
medications.dose_route_frequency: massage into the scalp, leave on for ~5 minutes, then rinse; use as directed on the product (source note advises leaving 5 minutes before rinsing)

medications.indication_rationale: There is no cellulitis or bacterial infection — the eruption is symmetrical, greasy-scaled, afebrile and chronic-relapsing. Systemic antibiotics would be an error of commission driven by the dramatic presentation.

medications.drug_name: potent / fluorinated topical corticosteroid on the face
medications.indication_rationale: Potent/fluorinated steroids on facial skin risk atrophy, telangiectasia, rosacea-like and perioral dermatitis; only a MILD steroid for a short course is appropriate on the face.
Systemic steroids are not warranted for a localised benign seborrhoeic flare and carry disproportionate risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Switch from harsh alkaline soaps to a gentle, pH-balanced cleanser and stop scrubbing the flakes; pat rather than rub.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Alkaline soap and friction strip the skin barrier and aggravate the inflammation; gentle cleansing lets the barrier recover.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Address the current work stress where possible (sleep, workload, relaxation strategies) as a flare trigger.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Physiological stress is a recognised trigger for seborrhoeic dermatitis flares.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a cool-mist humidifier to counteract dry, heavily heated indoor air.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cold, dry indoor air aggravates the skin barrier; humidification is a simple environmental modification.

patient_education_points.point: This is a common, benign, long-term skin condition where the immune system over-reacts to a normal yeast that lives in the oily areas of everyone's skin — it is not an infection you caught and it is not lupus.

The antifungal cream and shampoo reduce the yeast that drives the flare, while a SHORT course of a mild steroid cream calms the burning and redness — use the steroid only briefly and only on the face as directed.
Do not scrub or pick the flakes off — friction and harsh soap make it worse. Wash gently with a pH-balanced cleanser.
Leave the medicated shampoo on the scalp for about 5 minutes before rinsing so it has time to work.

The burning and redness usually settle within 3 to 5 days and the scaling over 1 to 2 weeks; because the condition is chronic and relapsing, expect to use intermittent maintenance treatment long term.

follow_up_plan.interval: review with the regular GP in about 14 days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response to topical therapy, resolution of the flare, and establishment of a long-term intermittent maintenance skin-care regimen; reconsider the diagnosis if not responding.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any of the red-flag features (skin becomes hot, deep, very painful or spreads rapidly over one cheek, or fever/chills develop), new joint pain / mouth ulcers / photosensitivity, steroid-induced skin thinning, or no improvement after two weeks of treatment.

must_include_items: Recognise a benign inflammatory greasy-scaled facial/scalp rash in a sebaceous distribution and reassure the patient it is not lupus or an emergency
Recommend a topical antifungal (e.g. ketoconazole cream + medicated shampoo) as first-line
Recommend a SHORT course of a MILD topical corticosteroid for the inflamed face, used sparingly
Advise stopping harsh alkaline soap / scrubbing and switching to a gentle pH-balanced cleanser
Give explicit safety-netting for the cellulitis and autoimmune red flags
Arrange routine GP review (about 14 days) without over-escalating
acceptable_alternatives: Alternative topical antifungal (e.g. another azole, ciclopirox) or an antifungal/anti-dandruff shampoo containing selenium sulfide or zinc pyrithione
A different mild topical corticosteroid or a topical calcineurin inhibitor (e.g. pimecrolimus) as a steroid-sparing option for the face
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net the facial-cellulitis red flags (hot, deep, painful, unilateral spread, fever)
Failing to safety-net the systemic-autoimmune red flags (joint pain, mouth ulcers, photosensitivity, sparing malar rash)
Failing to advise stopping harsh soap / scrubbing
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a classic benign flare to urgent care or the emergency department
Prescribing oral/systemic antibiotics for a non-infective rash
Recommending a potent/fluorinated topical steroid or an open-ended steroid course on the face
Confirming or excluding lupus / cellulitis with false certainty remotely, or ordering low-yield serology on the basis of the patient's fear alone
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (1) recognising the benign nature and NOT over-triaging, (2) first-line topical antifungal plus short mild topical steroid and gentle skin-care advice, and (3) conservative red-flag safety-netting for the cellulitis and autoimmune mimics. Over-escalation of this classic presentation, or systemic antibiotic/oral-steroid prescribing, is a scoring failure.
